coredata.dc:title: Bioimaging-guided discrimination of normal/cancer cells using Ag+-mediated red fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Farid
Xu, Weiqing
Muzammal Hussain, Muhammad
Ullah Khan, Waheed
Xiong, Hai
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) serves as a significant cancer-related biomarker that plays a pivotal role in preserving intracellular homeostasis. However, the structural and functional similarities between GSH and other biothioles (cysteine and homocysteine), make it difficult to distinguish GSH from them. In this study, we have developed a novel fluorescent probe utilizing silver-mediated red emissive carbon dots (RCDs@Ag+) for the discrimination of cancer cells from normal cells through the bioimaging of glutathione (GSH). The fluorescence intensity of RCDs is quenched significantly upon the addition of Ag+ (50 µM) and is recovered 95 % with the identical concentration of added GSH, without such effect occurring in cysteine and homocysteine. The fluorescence lifetime of the RCDs was found to be approximately 7.022 ns and the quantum yield was determined to be approximately 9.24 %. Further, RCDs@Ag+ are suitable for selectively detecting intracellular GSH due to their excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity in normal NIH3T3 and HeLa cells. Most significantly, RCDs@Ag+ may be used as an excellent approach to spot cancer cells overexpressing GSH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red Fluorescence probe
Glutathione
Cell imaging
Cell recognition

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing mechanism for detection of Ag+ and GSH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Fields of Universities in Guangdong Province
Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Special Project of Key Fields of Universities in Guangdong Province, China (2021ZDZX2047 to HX), the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China (JCYJ20210324095607021 to HX).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of RCDs using various spectroscopic techniques (a) HR-TEM image of RCDs (Insect of Fig. 1a shows the size distribution and lattice fringes), (b) FTIR spectrum of RCDs, (c) Full XPS spectrum, (d) High-resolution C 1 s spectrum, (e) High-resolution N 1 s spectrum, and (f) High-resolution O 1 S spectrum of RCDs.

(a) Excitation and emission photoluminescence spectrum of RCDs, (b) wavelength independent emission spectrum of RCDs upon different excitation wavelengths 520–630 nm, (c) lifetime decay curve of RCDs, (d) Thermal stability of RCDs, (e) effect of pH on the stability of RCDs, and (f) photostability of RCDs under UV 365 nm lamp illumination.

(a) Change in the FL intensity of RCDs upon interaction with various metal cations (100 µM), (b) quenching in the FL intensity of RCDs as a function of Ag+ concentrations (1–100 µM), (c) selectivity of RCDs towards Ag+ in the presence of a variety of metal cations 1 = Al3+, 2 = Ca2+, 3 = Cd2+, 4 = Cu2+, 5 = Cr+2, 6 = Fe3+, 7 = Hg2+, 8 = K+, 9 = Mg2+, 10 = Mn2+, 11 = Na+, 12 = Ni2+, 13 = Pb2+, 14 = Zn2+, 15 = Mixture, (d) change in the FL intensity of RCDs@Ag+ in presence of a variety of neutral analytes, (e) effect of different concentrations of GSH on FL recovery of RCDs@Ag+, (f) selectivity of RCDs@Ag+ towards GSH in the presence of other neutral analytes. 1 = ascorbic acid, 2 = alanine, 3 = arginine, 4 = cytosine, 5 = cystine, 6 = homocysteine, 7 = guanine, 8 = lysine, 9 = methionine, 10 = proline, 11 = threonine and 12 = mixture.
CLSM images of NIH3T3 cells using RCDs@Ag+ as fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for the intracellular detection of GSH. Green channel, λex = 488 nm; Red channel, λex = 561 nm. The scale bars are 20 μm.
CLSM images of NIH3T3 and HeLa cells incubated with RCDs@Ag+. Green channel, λex = 488 nm; Red channel, λex = 561 nm. The scale bars are 20 μm.
The schematic diagram for the synthesis of RCDs from citric acid and aminoquinoline.
Schematic representation of RCDs-based nanoprobe for the detection of Ag+ and GSH.

head.ce:title: Bioimaging-guided discrimination of normal/cancer cells using Ag+-mediated red fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) serves as a significant cancer-related biomarker that plays a pivotal role in preserving intracellular homeostasis. However, the structural and functional similarities between GSH and other biothioles (cysteine and homocysteine), make it difficult to distinguish GSH from them. In this study, we have developed a novel fluorescent probe utilizing silver-mediated red emissive carbon dots (RCDs@Ag+) for the discrimination of cancer cells from normal cells through the bioimaging of glutathione (GSH). The fluorescence intensity of RCDs is quenched significantly upon the addition of Ag+ (50 µM) and is recovered 95 % with the identical concentration of added GSH, without such effect occurring in cysteine and homocysteine. The fluorescence lifetime of the RCDs was found to be approximately 7.022 ns and the quantum yield was determined to be approximately 9.24 %. Further, RCDs@Ag+ are suitable for selectively detecting intracellular GSH due to their excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity in normal NIH3T3 and HeLa cells. Most significantly, RCDs@Ag+ may be used as an excellent approach to spot cancer cells overexpressing GSH.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is one of the most abundant biothiol in living cells (approximately 1–10 mM), and it is involved in various biological processes such as homeostasis, metabolism, signal transmission, and gene regulation [1–3]. The abnormal concentration of GSH is closely associated with various diseases including cancer, acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS), cardiovascular problems, liver damage, aging, diabetes, etc. [4–8]. Monitoring intracellular GSH levels in real-time is crucial due to the potential negative impacts on human health resulting from perturbations or imbalances in its concentration. GSH, a cancer-related biomarker, plays a vital role in maintaining intracellular homeostasis. However, discriminating GSH from structurally and functionally similar biological thiols like Cys and Hcys poses challenges, making in vivo differentiation problematic [9]. To date, various analytical methodologies have been used for the selective detection of GSH, such as Electrochemistry [10], Liquid chromatography [11], Colorimetry [12], Mass spectroscopy [13], Raman spectroscopy [14], Fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy [3,15], etc. Although most of these methods are reliable, highly selective, accurate, and useful for monitoring the concentration of intracellular GSH, they often have several drawbacks, such as complex sample preparation steps, expensive instrumentation requirements, the need for highly trained professionals, time-consuming and laborious processes, etc. Therefore, there is a need for the development of simple, selective, time-saving, and cost-effective methods for the selective detection of GSH in a complex environment.

With their high selectivity, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and remarkable optical characteristics, CDs-based fluorescent imaging has already been established as an exceptional sensing platform for the detection of cations [16], anions [17–19], organic pollutants [20,21], and other biologically important analytes [22–24]. In most cases, the detection principle of a CDs-based fluorescent sensor is a simple turn-off or turn-on process towards a particular analyte, which is susceptible to various environmental and biological factors, leading to false positive signals [25]. To overcome this issue, CDs can be incorporated with FL quenchers such as Cu2+ Hg2+, Fe3+, MnO4 −, etc. in the presence of the targeted analyte, resulting in the novel “on-off-on” switchable sensors due to their stronger affinity or reactivity towards quenchers [26,27]. These indirect “on-off-on” FL sensors offer better analytical sensitivity than that of an “on-off” switch in the detection of pharmaceuticals, biomolecules, food toxins, and environmental pollutants. Up till now, several articles have been published related to the detection of GSH metal-mediated CDs through an “on-off-on” mechanism [28–31]. For example, Dan Chang and colleagues have reported the successful synthesis of a ratiometric fluorescent probe, NSCDs@Hg2+, which enables discrimination between GSH and other biothiols such as Cys and Hcys. The interaction between Hg2+ and NSCDs leads to the aggregation of NSCDs nanoparticles, but the strong binding affinity between GSH and Hg2+ reverses this process and results in dispersion [32]. While numerous published methods have shown high selectivity in detecting GSH, most of them rely on blue emissive CDs, ratiometric detection, and have relatively high detection limits. This limitation hampers their potential application in biological assays. On the other hand, on–off-on fluorescents probes offers high signal-to-noise ratio as the ”off“ state of the probe minimizes background FL, facilitating a distinct differentiation between the presence and absence of the target analyte. This characteristic renders on–off-on probes particularly advantageous for real-time monitoring of dynamic processes. Their reversible and switchable response enables continuous tracking of target analyte levels, making them ideal for studying time-dependent changes and dynamic biological processes.

In this study, we have introduced a novel approach utilizing Ag+-mediated responsive RCDs for the discrimination of normal cells and cancer cells based on glutathione (GSH) bioimaging. The RCDs were synthesized using 8-aminoquinoline and citric acid through a hydrothermal approach and characterized using various spectroscopic techniques such as TEM, XPS, XRD, and FTIR. The FL intensity of RCDs was quenched upon treatment with Ag+, while other tested analytes did not produce any detectable alteration in the FL intensity. Interestingly the quenched FL of RCDs@Ag+ recovered 95 % upon treatment with 50 µM of GSH. The FL recovery is directly proportional to the concentration of GSH with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 µM. Additionally, RCDs were found to be non-toxic for NIH3T3 and HeLa cells up to 500 µg/mL. The RCDs@Ag+ probe is successfully employed for the detection of GSH in NIH3T3 cells and can be also used to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells due to its imaging ability for different GSH concentrations. The RCDs can be developed as a diagnostic tool by recognizing endogenous GSH in live cells, and have the potential to be used for the discrimination of normal and cancer cells.

Citric acid and 8-amino quinoline were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai China), whereas other metal salts including AgNO3, AlCl3, CaCl2, CdCl2, CrCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, MgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, and Zn(NO3)2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Amoxicillin, ampicillin, ascorbic acid, chloramphenicol, cystine, glutathione, kanamycin, streptomycin, and oxytetracycline were purchased from Macklin. The UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), while the FL spectra were measured using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI). The particle size and morphology of RCDs were analyzed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM JEOL F-200 operating at 200 kV), and the presence of different functional groups was explored through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum using FT-IR Spectrometer (PerkinElmer). The elemental composition of the synthesized RCDs was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo-VG Scientific ESCALab 250 microprobe equipment with a monochromatic Al K-alpha source (1361 eV) (Thermo Fisher). The cell imaging was performed by using Nikon confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) A1R + Ti.

500 mg each of 8-aminoquinoline and citric acid were dissolved in 10 mL of formamide and sonicated for 20 min. Subsequently, the resultant solution was then introduced into a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 hrs. Upon completion of the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to ambient temperature through natural means. The resultant solution was then diluted with water and centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min to remove any solid particles. This purification process was repeated thrice to eliminate any solid impurities. The resulting solution of RCDs was then filtered using a Millipore 0.22 µM filter paper and dialyzed for 24 hrs using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) to obtain a dark red solution of purified RCDs. The yield of synthesized RCDs is around 38 %. Finally, the pure RCDs were freeze-dried and preserved at 4 °C in the refrigerator for further use.

For the recognition experiments, a 1 mL solution containing various metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Cr+2, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) and neutral analytes (amoxicillin, ampicillin, chloramphenicol, cystine, glutathione, kanamycin, streptomycin, and oxytetracycline) at a concentration of 50 µM, was treated with 1 mL of RCDs at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. Subsequently, alterations in FL were evaluated using an excitation wavelength of 600 nm. For the selectivity experiments, RCDs were exposed to various other metal ions and neutral analytes in the presence of Ag+. Subsequently, the alterations in FL were recorded and analyzed. To determine the LOD various concentrations of Ag+ (1–100 µM) were treated with constant concentration of RCDs (0.2 mg/mL) and changes in the FL intensity were recorded. The FL recovery experiments were performed by successive addition of GSH (1–100 µM) to RCDs@Ag+ solution, and the fluorescence spectrums were recorded.

The cell cytotoxicity assay involved evaluating the effects of RCDs on normal (NIH3T3) and cancer cell lines (HeLa cells) using the MMT assay. NIH-3 T3 cells were cultivated in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % double antibodies, followed by overnight incubation at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 environment. The original DMEM was then removed, and 100 µL of 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) DMEM containing varying doses of RCDs and RCDs@Ag+ (0, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500 µg/mL) were added to the wells. The cells were then incubated for an additional 24 h. The cells were cultured for 4 hrs after adding 20 µL of 5.0 mg/mL MTT reagent to each well and then assessed for cell viability of RCDs/RCDs@Ag+. Upon removal of the growth medium, 150 µL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was introduced to render the formazan salt soluble. The optical density (OD) of each well was subsequently measured at 490 nm using the BioTek Synergy equipment.

To conduct cell imaging assays, NIH3T3 cells were cultured on a glass bottom dish and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2 overnight. Afterward, NIH3T3 and HeLa cells were exposed to a 200 µg/mL RCDs solution and incubated at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 for 24 hrs. Subsequently, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) buffer and 2 mL of fresh DMEM solution was added to the glass bottom dish before cell imaging. CLSM with varying excitation wavelengths was utilized to examine the cells. After incubation for 4 hrs, 10 µg/mL of Ag+ was added to each well and the cells were observed using CLSM. For the in-vitro detection of GSH, NIH3T3 cells incubated with RCDs@Ag+ were treated with 50 µg of GSH, and changes in cell FL were recorded.

The RCDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal approach that employed 8-amino quinoline and citric acid as precursors as depicted in scheme 1 . The synthesized CDs were then characterized using a variety of spectroscopic techniques such as HR-TEM, FTIR, XPS, XRD, and NMR. The average size and morphology of the RCDs were analyzed through HR-TEM. Fig. 1 a shows that the CDs exhibited a spherical shape and were uniformly dispersed. The diameter distribution of the CDs ranged from 2.4 to 8.6 nm with an average diameter of 4.36 nm (Fig. 1a insect). The HR-TEM image of CDs (inset of Fig. 1a) displays well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [33]. The presence of different functional groups was confirmed by FTIR analysis. As depicted in Fig. 1b, the peaks at 3326.5 cm−1 and 3145.6 cm−1 are attributed to -O-H and -N-H stretching vibrations. The band around 2803.2 cm−1 is due to -C-H stretching vibrations. The absorption band around 1660.19 cm−1 is assigned to the -C = O/-C = N group. Furthermore, the stretching vibration bands of -C = C and –NH bending were observed at 1607.2 cm−1 and 1587.1 cm−1, respectively. The absorption bands of -C-N, -C-O, and -C-C are also present in the FTIR spectrum of CDs. The FTIR analysis reveals that the surface of the synthesized CDs is rich in –NH2, –COOH, and –OH-based functional groups. The elemental composition of the RCDs was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Fig. 1c represents the overall survey spectrum of RCDs, demonstrating three main peaks for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s at 285 eV, 398 eV, and 531 eV, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) showed characteristic peaks at 284.30 eV for -C-C/C = C, 285.37 eV for -C-N/C-O, and 287.13 eV for -C = O, respectively. Additionally, the N 1 s spectrum of RCDs showed peaks at 398.34 eV and 399.51 eV for amine/amide and pyrrolic N (Fig. 1e). The O 1 s spectrum of the CDs showed peaks at 532.25 eV for -C = O and 530.90 eV for -O-C-O, respectively (Fig. 1f). Fig. S1 represents the content of C, N, and O, which is 66.15 %, 26.89 %, and 7.08 %, respectively. These findings suggest that newly synthesized RCDs have a higher nitrogen content compared to most reported CDs. The presence of different functional groups on the surface of CDs was confirmed by FTIR analysis. Furthermore, XRD analysis was performed to determine the purity and core structure of the RCDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the XRD pattern of RCDs exhibited a sharp diffraction band at 2θ = 26.56 for representing the 022-crystal lattice spacing of carbon-based material [34]. Furthermore, the presence of an aromatic core and different nitrogen was also confirmed by 1H NMR and 15N NMR spectroscopy (Fig. S3 and S4). The peak at 10.76 ppm represents COOH groups of RCDs. The peaks at 9.12, 8.91, and 8.60 ppm represent the aromatic -C = C- functional groups of aromatic rings. Further, the 15N NMR spectrum showed characteristics peaks at 279.22 ppm (Pyridine N), 112.86 ppm (amide), and 76.39 ppm (aromatic –NH2) confirming the presence of different nitrogen-containing functional groups of RCDs.

To understand the optical characteristics and the FL mechanism of the carbon dots (CDs), we conducted investigations into UV − vis absorption, FL spectra, quantum yield (QY), and FL lifetime. Fig. S5 shows that the RCDs exhibited a major absorption band at around 260 and 320 nm, which can be attributed to the π- π and n- π* transitions, respectively. Moreover, a broad absorption band in the range of 520–600 nm was observed, most likely due to extended conjugation of the CDs’ core structure. Fig. 2 a demonstrates the excitation and emission spectrum of quinoline-derived RCDs. Generally, most CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission due to irregular size and the presence of various functional groups that possess different emissive traps. The red emission of carbon dots is most probably due to the presence of graphite nitrogen-rich skeleton of CDs [35]. However, the newly synthesized RCDs showed excitation-independent emission at 655 nm when exited in a wide range of wavelengths (520–630 nm) (Fig. 2b). The FL intensity of the CDs increased linearly with an increase in wavelength from 520 to 610 nm, and the emission wavelength remained consistent. The strongest emission was observed at 655 nm when excited at 610 nm. This excitation-independent emission is most likely due to the uniform size distribution and uniform surface state of the synthesized CDs. By employing rhodamine B as the reference, we determined the quantum yield (QY) of RCDs in an aqueous solution to be approximately 9.24 %. Additionally, the CDs demonstrated an average FL lifetime of approximately 7.885 ns (Fig. 2c).

The stability of CDs is crucial for various real-time environmental and biological assays. To assess the stability of RCDs, several parameters were optimized, including temperature, electrolyte concentration, and pH. The stability of RCDs in the presence of various concentrations of the electrolyte solution was evaluated by recording the variations in the FL intensity. The results indicate that the luminescence of RCDs did not change significantly with increasing NaCl concentration up to 0.5 M (Fig. S6). These findings suggest that RCDs are stable even in high-concentration electrolyte media. To evaluate thermal stability, an aqueous solution of RCDs was heated to different temperatures (25–100 °C), and changes in the FL intensity were recorded. As shown in Fig. 2d, the FL intensity did not significantly fluctuate at high temperatures, indicating the remarkable thermal stability of RCDs at elevated temperatures. To ensure the stability of carbon dots in each batch, we controlled the synthesis parameters, purified, and characterized the carbon dots, and stored them under appropriate conditions. Specifically, we optimized the synthesis parameters, such as reaction temperature, and time, to ensure a consistent size distribution and surface chemistry of the carbon dots. We stored RCDs in a dry, cool, and dark environment. We also stored the carbon dots in a buffer solution for biological assays to prevent degradation or agglomeration.

Furthermore, the stability of CDs was also evaluated by investing their response to different pH values ranging from 2 to 12. The pH of the CDs solution was adjusted using a dilute aqueous solution of HCl and NaOH. It is clear from Fig. 2e that RCDs showed irregular trends in acidic, neutral, and basic media. In the acidic medium (pH 2–5), a slight blue shift was observed, while the FL intensity decreased slightly, most likely due to the protonation of surface functional groups. Conversely, in the basic media (pH 10–12), the FL intensity decreased significantly with the bathochromic shift. In a neutral media (pH 6–8), RCDs exhibited the highest FL intensity without any significant shift in the wavelength. This strong FL in the neutral pH range demonstrates that RCDs are an excellent candidate for biological applications. The photostability of RCDs was determined by continuously irradiating them with a xenon UV lamp for 60 min at 365 nm, and the changes in FL were measured as presented in Fig. 2f. The FL intensity of RCDs did not change significantly upon UV irradiations, demonstrating their excellent photostability. The kinetic stability of RCDs was also tested by recording the FL over time, up to 60 days as depicted in Fig. S7. The FL of RCDs decreased slightly after 60 days, indicating excellent kinetic stability. These results highlight the significance of RCDs in various real-time environmental and biological assays, where stability is a critical factor to consider.

The complexation of CDs with metal ions plays a critical role in numerous applications. The stability of these complexes is influenced by the pH and ionic strength of the surrounding medium. We also investigated the stability of the RCDs@Ag+ complex under various pH conditions and different concentrations of NaCl. The experimental results, presented in Fig. S8, demonstrate that the RCDs@Ag+ complex remains stable within a neutral pH range (pH 5 to pH 8). However, the complex undergoes disruption at highly acidic and basic pH values. The deformation process of the complex is attributed to protonation and deprotonation phenomena occurring in the highly acidic and basic media. Considering the distinct electrostatic attraction between chloride ions (Cl-) and silver ions (Ag+), a series of experiments were carried out utilizing varying concentrations of NaCl (ranging from 1 mM to 300 mM) to evaluate the durability of RCDs@Ag+. Fig. S9 displays the findings, indicating that this intricate assembly maintains remarkable stability even when exposed to NaCl concentrations as elevated as 100 mM. Significantly, when subjected to a 150 mM NaCl concentration (within the typical range found in bodily fluids, approximately 135–145 mM), a slight decrease in stability was observed. However, as the NaCl concentration continues to rise, the assembly experiences a gradual decline in stability. This can be attributed to the gradual formation of silver chloride (AgCl). All sensing experiments were conducted at a pH of 7.0, while the bioimaging experiments were performed using a phosphate buffer with a pH of 7.6.

After the successful synthesis and characterization of RCDs, their sensing potential towards metal cations and neutral analytes was evaluated using UV–visible and FL spectroscopy. In metal ion recognition experiments, 100 µM solution of various metal cations such as Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Cr+2, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were treated with RCDs solution and changes in FL intensity were recorded. The results presented in Fig. 3 a demonstrate that the FL intensity of RCDs was substantially quenched upon the addition of Ag+, while most of the other tested metal ions had minimal impact on the FL intensity. The interactions between metal ions and RCDs are multifaceted and can depend on a combination of factors, including the overall surface chemistry, coordination chemistry, size, and electronic properties of both the carbon dots and the metal ions. The distinct fluorescence quenching behavior of Ag+ with RCDs arises from Ag+ high effective nuclear charge, strong electron capture affinity, favorable ion stability after electron capture, and efficient energy transfer via FRET. Other metal ions, such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, etc., do not capture electrons from carbon dots as effectively as Ag+, resulting in weaker or no fluorescence quenching. Specifically, the quenching mechanism involves electron transfer, where RCDs act as photosensitizing electron donors, while Ag+ serves as electron acceptors. Upon absorbing sufficient photon energy, the excited electrons of RCDs migrate to Ag+, thus reducing nonradiative electron/hole recombination in RCDs. This charge transfer process effectively hinders the return of photoinduced electrons to the RCDs, resulting in efficient quenching of the fluorescence of RCDs. These findings indicate that RCDs possess specific coordination with Ag+ ions as compared to other tested metal ions, making them suitable for use as a nanoprobe in detecting Ag+ even in the presence of various other analytes in a complex system. Furthermore, the study also investigated the interaction of RCDs with various biologically important analytes, as depicted in Fig. S10. The FL intensity of RCDs remained unaffected, with no significant alterations in intensity or wavelength observed when exposed to several biologically important analytes such as ascorbic acid, amoxicillin, ampicillin, chloramphenicol, cystine, glutathione, kanamycin, streptomycin, and oxytetracycline (50 µM). This suggests that the FL response of RCDs is specifically triggered by the presence of Ag+ and remains insensitive to these other biologically relevant analytes. Moreover, the reaction time for the recognition of Ag+ was optimized, as shown in Fig. S11. The FL quenching of RCDs was found to exceed 90 % within one minute of Ag+ interaction, and after that, it remained relatively constant. Moreover, validation of the RCDs@Ag+-based nanocomposite's formation was also established through analysis via UV–visible absorption spectrum and XRD techniques, as illustrated in Fig. S12 and Fig. S13. The emergence of a novel absorption band at approximately 550 nm due to the introduction of Ag+ is shown in Fig. S12. In the XRD spectrum of RCDs@Ag+, distinctive peaks corresponding to Ag were identified at 2θ values of 39.16 and 44.50, in addition to a broad graphite signal at 2θ = 26.50 (depicted in Fig. S13). The XRD spectrum of RCDs in the presence of other tested metal cations (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) under similar experimental conditions did not show any detectable change in the 2θ value of RCDs. These findings collectively affirm the creation of a stable complex between RCDs and Ag+. This rapid response of RCDs to Ag+ further highlights their potential as a nanoprobe for the efficient and quick detection of Ag+ in complex systems.

The effect of different concentrations of Ag+ on the FL quenching of RCDs was studied using concentration-dependent experiments to determine the linear range and LOD calculation. These experiments demonstrated that the addition of Ag+ in the range 1–100 µM resulted in a gradual decrease in the FL intensity of RCDs, and the quenching of FL was directly proportional to the concentration of Ag+ with R2 = 0.9975 (Fig. 3b). The LOD for Ag+ was calculated using the standard deviation of the blank and the slope of the straight-line equation using the formula LOD = 3.3 × S.D./slope and found to be 0.15 µM.

The selectivity of a fluorescent probe towards a particular analyte in the presence of other similar analytes is an essential criterion when it is applied to practical applications. The proposed sensor selectivity for Ag+ was assessed under similar experimental conditions, alongside the presence of various metal cations (each at a concentration of 100 µM) including Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Cr+2, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+. The FL intensity of RCDs@Ag+, when exposed to these different metal ions, was observed to be nearly identical to that observed with Ag+ alone. This observation indicates that the presence of the other metal ions did not interfere with the detection process. Consequently, it can be concluded that the RCDs-based fluorescent probe exhibits remarkable selectivity for Ag+ and is suitable for detecting Ag+ even when other metal cations are present (as shown in Fig. 3c). The quenched FL of the RCDs@Ag+ complex is almost recovered 95 % upon the addition of GSH 50 µM. Furthermore, this fluorescent turn-off probe for Ag+ can be further used as an on–off-on sensor for the detection of GSH.

Biothiols such as Cys, Hcys, and GSH have a high affinity for several metal ions such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, etc. They can remove metal ions from the surface of CDs and recover the quenched FL [23,36]. Therefore, FL recovery experiments of RCDs@Ag+ were conducted in the presence of several neutral analytes (100 µM) such as ascorbic acid, alanine, arginine, chloramphenicol, Cys, Hcys, guanine, lysine, methionine, threonine, and oxytetracycline. Fig. 3d shows that the addition of GSH recovered nearly 95 % FL of RCDs, while other tested analytes did not produce any significant change in the FL intensity of RCDs@Ag+. The FL turn-on response of RCDs@Ag+ in the presence of GSH is most probably due to the strong interaction between the thiol and amino group of the GSH with Ag+ on the surface of RCDs. FL recovery is a quick process and almost 95 % of FL is recovered within one minute (Fig. S14). The obtained results demonstrate that GSH has a stronger affinity for Ag+ ions and is easy to remove Ag+ from the surface of RCDs compared to RCDs, leading to FL recovery for the quenched FL system. Interestingly, other thiols such as Cys and Hcys did not produce any significant change in FL intensity, indicating the excellent selectivity of RCDs@Ag+ towards GSH detection in the presence of other biologically important analytes. Concentration-dependent experiments were performed to determine the limit of GSH detection using an RCDs@Ag+-based fluorescent probe. Different concentrations of GSH ranging from 0.1 to 50 µM were subjected to treatment with RCDs@Ag+. The FL intensity was measured and depicted in Fig. 3e. The recovery in the FL intensity is directly proportional to the GSH concentration over a wide range of concentrations, with a linear correlation factor of R2 = 0.9976. The LOD for GSH was determined by using the slope of the straight-line equation and was found to be 0.12 µM. The ability of RCDs@Ag+ to distinguish GSH from other likely coexisting analytes, such as amino acids, and other important biological analytes was explored. Given that the goal of RCDs@Ag+ is to identify GSH selectively in the presence of other biothiols such as Cys and Hcys. The results of this investigation in the presence of interfering analytes (1 mM) were included in Fig. 3f. It was discovered that other analytes did not produce any significant change in the FL response of GSH. The outcomes showed that the RCDs@Ag+ had a high degree of GSH selectivity compared to other similar biothiols such as Cys, Hcys, and other biologically important analytes. More importantly, the probe RCDs@Ag+ can discriminate GSH from other biothiols including Cys and Hcys. We also examined the combined effect of both analytes (Ag + and GSH) and observed a slight decrease in the FL intensity of RCDs upon their addition. These findings suggest that RCDs@Ag+ can be employed as a nanoprobe for real-time monitoring of GSH in environmental and biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for detection of Ag+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: The FL quenching of CDs is usually through static quenching, dynamic quenching, inner filter effect (IFE), electron transfer (ET), and Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Particularly, dynamic quenching and FRET process reduce the FL decay life of CDs. To corroborate these interactions, we conducted UV–vis absorption spectrometry experiments. The observed shift in the absorption peaks of RCDs upon the addition of Ag+ (450 nm to 650 nm) confirms the formation of RCDs@Ag+ complexes (Fig. S12). Furthermore, upon introducing GSH, the absorption peaks of RCDs reverted to their initial position, further supporting the dissociation of Ag+ from the surface of RCDs. Fig. S15 shows that the decay lifetime of RCDs@Ag+ (7.02 ns) and RCDs@Ag++GSH (7.32 ns) does not change much upon complex formation, demonstrating that the PL quenching is not due to dynamic quenching or FRET process. So, the FL quenching of RCDs upon addition is most probably static quenching. According to the obtained results, the process is the creation of a nonfluorescent complex between RCDs and Ag+, which causes the occurrence of static quenching [37]. The introduction of GSH into the system plays a critical role. The strong binding affinity of GSH with metal ions facilitates the removal of Ag+ from the surface of RCDs, consequently restoring the FL emission [38–41]. Being a thiophilic metal ion, Ag+ also prefers biothiol with a high affinity. The competitive coordination mechanism can be attributed to the FL recovery of the RCDs@Ag+ system by GSH [23,42,43]. The effect of size and nucleophilic ability of GSH towards RCDs@Ag+ may contribute to the discrimination of GSH from Hcys and Cys [44]. Due to its greater binding capacity with Ag+, GSH may be competitively trapped Ag+ from the surface of RCDs and the FL intensity of RCDs recovered [45] (Scheme 2 ). The FL recovery is directly proportional to the concentration of GSH added. As a result, RCDs may be used as a powerful tool for FL recognition of Ag+ and GSH with a reversible response. The interaction between RCDs and Ag+ was further investigated through FTIR spectroscopic analysis. In the FTIR spectrum of RCDs@Ag+, certain characteristic bands underwent noticeable changes upon interaction with Ag+. The –OH (hydroxyl) and –NH2 (amine) bands, originally observed at 3326.5 and 3145.6 cm−1, respectively, were observed to almost vanish or significantly decrease in intensity upon interaction with Ag+. This suggests that the presence of Ag+ led to the alteration or reduction of these functional groups on the surface of RCDs. Furthermore, the intensity of the -C = O (carbonyl) band at 1660.19 cm−1 and the -C = C band at 1606.20 cm−1 also experienced significant reductions upon interaction with Ag+. Additionally, there was a slight shift in the peak positions of these bands, indicating a change in the local environment or bonding characteristics (Fig. S16).

The biocompatibility of CDs is a crucial factor to consider when planning for their potential applications in cellular imaging and other biological applications. To assess the toxicity of newly synthesized RCDs towards normal and cancer cells, cytotoxicity experiments were conducted using an MTT assay. As illustrated in Fig. S17, the cell survival rate was more than 80 % for NIH3T3 cells and over 85 % for HeLa cells, even at the highest concentration of 500 µg/mL of RCDs. Interestingly, the RCDs were found to be more compatible with HeLa cells compared to normal NIH3T3 cells. These findings suggest that synthesized RCDs are biocompatible and can be used for cell imaging and other bio-related applications. Furthermore, the toxicity of RCDs in the presence of Ag+ was also evaluated to use RCDs@Ag+ for the detection of GSH in live cells. As depicted in Fig. S18 RCDs were compatible with NIH3T3 and HeLa cell lines in the presence of Ag+ (1–20 µM). The survival rate of both normal and cancer cells was more than 80 % even in the presence of 20 µM of Ag+ ion. These results demonstrate that RCDs@Ag+-based nanocomposite is biocompatible with NIH3T3 and HeLa cells and easily employed as a fluorescent probe for the detection of GSH.

The synthesized RCDs were subjected to all imaging applications due to their excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. Various parameters related to cell imaging application were optimized, such as the effective concentration of CDs, optimal time for cell imaging, and optimal concentration of Ag+ and GSH. To determine the optimal concentration of CDs for cell imaging application, NIH3T3 cells were incubated with different concentrations (50, 100, 200 µg/mL) for 4 hrs, and cell imaging was performed as depicted in Fig. S19. As seen from the results with increasing concentration of RCDs, cell brightness also increases, indicating that more RCDs entered the cells. Therefore, the optimal concentration of CDs was selected as 200 µg/mL for imaging applications. Furthermore, the effect of incubation time on the FL intensity of cells was evaluated using an optimal concentration of CDs for different time intervals (30 min-32 hrs). As illustrated in Fig. S20, the FL intensity of cells is directly proportional to the incubation time up to 8 h after, which remains almost constant. Thus, 4 hrs were selected as the optimal time for in vitro detection of Ag+ and GSH. Under the laser irradiation at 488 and 561 nm; the intracellular region of NIH3T3 cells exhibits green and red dual-channel FL in the presence of RCDs (200 µg/mL), as depicted in Fig. S21. The pre-treated HeLa and NIH3T3 cells were incubated with 200 µg/mL for 4 hrs and cell imaging experiments were performed. Control cells (in this case PBS without RCDs) showed almost no detectable FL signal, while the sample cells (HeLa and NIH3T3 treated with RCDs) showed excellent bright FL in the green and red channels. These results reveal that RCDs can be applied for living cell imaging, demonstrating the excellent biological application prospect.

RCDs possess outstanding optical properties, photostability, and biocompatibility, making them an ideal candidate for in vitro detection of Ag+ and GSH in NIH3T3 cells. The addition of 5 µM of Ag+ to RCDs pre-treated NIH3T3 cells resulted in a significant decrease in green and red FL, as shown in Fig. 4 . The decrease in FL intensity in the green and red channels is directly proportional to the concentration of added Ag+ (1–20 µM), as depicted in Fig. S22. These results are comparable with FL detection of Ag+, which showed a significant reduction of FL. Additionally, the confocal microscopy validated the FL turn-on response of RCDs@Ag+ upon the addition of GSH. The addition of GSH (50 µM) to the RCDs@Ag+ with a pre-treated NIH3T3 cells system resulted in a considerable FL restoration (Fig. 4). The cell FL recovery is directly proportional to the added concentration of GSH (Fig. S23). It is clear from Fig. S20 that as the concentration of GSH increases from 50 to 200 µM; the brightness of the cells also increases. Furthermore, the selectivity of RCDs@Ag+ towards GSH detection in normal cells in the presence of cystine was also evaluated using confocal microscopy. Under above mentioned experimental condition similar biothiol cystine was treated with RCDs@Ag+ and changes in the cell brightness were monitored using confocal microscopy. It can be seen from Fig. S24 that no such FL recovery was observed in the presence of cystine which authenticates the excellent selectivity of RCDs@Ag+-based nanoprobe for the detection of GSH in the presence of other related biological analytes. These findings suggest that newly synthesized RCDs are nontoxic for live cells and can detect Ag+ and GSH intracellularly with high selectivity without any interference from biological matrices.

It is well established that the GSH content in tumor cells is much higher in the mM range than in normal cells and varies between cancer cells [13,46]. Thus, RCDs@Ag+ as a responsive probe for GSH may have the potential to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells based on the large difference in GSH concentration. For cell recognition experiments 5 µM Ag+ was employed in combination with 200 µg/mL RCDs. To get more insight into cancer cell recognition potential we compared the FL imaging behaviour of RCDs@Ag+ incubated in NIH3T3 and HeLa. From Fig. 5 , it’s clear that the cancer cells (HeLa cells) exhibited much brighter FL than normal cells (NIH3T3 cells) upon incubation with RCDs@Ag+. The difference in the FL in HeLa cell is most probably because cancer cells contain a high concentration of GSH; which eradicate Ag+ from RCDs@Ag+ nanocomposite, as a result, RCDs showed excellent FL in HeLa cells (Fig. S25). On the other hand, the GSH concentration is not high enough to remove Ag+ from RCDs@Ag+ as a result the FL decreases significantly. The obtained results expressed that the probe RCDs@Ag+ can distinguish normal cells from cancer cells in the biological system. The outcomes suggest that the probe can detect exogenous/endogenous GSH in viable cells and discriminate cancer cells from normal cells in a ratiometric approach.

In this work, we developed a novel silver-mediated red emissive carbon dots (RCDs@Ag+) based fluorescent for discrimination of cancer cell/normal cells through GSH bioimaging. The addition of Ag+ effectively quenched the FL of RCDs and exhibited high sensitivity and sensitivity with a LOD of 0.15 μM. The quenched FL of RCDs@Ag+ is recovered almost 95 % upon the addition of GSH (50 μM). The LOD for GSH detection using RCDs@Ag+ was calculated to be 0.12 μM. Moreover, the RCDs were successfully employed for the intracellular detection of Ag+ and GSH in NIH3T3 and HeLa cells without any interference, due to their various advantages including exceptional stability, low toxicity, superior biocompatibility, and high sensitivity. Further, significantly confocal fluorescent microscopy demonstrates that the designed probe could be employed as a label-free fluorescent probe to distinguish a cancer cell from normal cells by detecting overexpressed GSH concentration. Our findings open a promising perspective for the development of CDs-based FL probes for environmental monitoring and biological analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Special Project of Key Fields of Universities in Guangdong Province, China (2021ZDZX2047 to HX), the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen, China (JCYJ20210324095607021 to HX).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147300.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
